title: Polymer clay ammonite pendant
subtitle: A spiral fossil-form pendant with carved chamber divisions
excerpt: A pendant shaped as an ammonite fossil, with a tight logarithmic spiral form and carved chamber suture lines. Polymer clay is sculpted by hand into the spiral shape, then the chamber lines are scored before curing. The finished pendant is approximately 35 mm diameter and is painted with metallic acrylic for an aged fossil effect.
sourceNotes: Ammonite form modelled from natural history reference; construction technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that cures hard in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.

content.text: An ammonite is an extinct cephalopod with a tightly coiled spiral shell divided into gas-filled chambers. The form is among the most recognisable in natural history collections. In

, the spiral is built by rolling a tapered rope and coiling it from the centre outward, then adding carved suture lines before curing. No kiln required. The finished pendant, painted with metallic acrylic, reads convincingly as a polished fossil specimen.

items.name: Acrylic paint, gold or copper metallic
Acrylic paint, dark brown or black

content.text: Condition the clay. Roll it into a tapered rope about 200 mm long, starting from 8 mm diameter at one end tapering to a point at the other. The thick end is the outer body whorl; the thin end is the tight inner winding. Starting from the thin end, coil the rope around itself on the wooden board, pressing each coil firmly against the one below. The spiral should lie flat. Work outward until you reach the thick end, which forms the last visible outer whorl.

Press the coiled spiral flat with the wooden board from the top to create an even thickness across the disc. The edge of the spiral should remain slightly rounded. The finished disc should be about 35 mm across and 8 mm thick at the outer whorl, tapering toward the centre.

Use the needle tool to score curved division lines radiating outward from the inner coil, crossing each whorl at intervals of about 5 mm. Real ammonite suture lines are complex and lobed; simplified curved lines are convincing at this scale. Score to a depth of about 1 mm without cutting through the clay.
Pierce a hole through the outer whorl near the top of the spiral with the needle tool. Bake flat on a dedicated oven liner at 110-130°C for 25 minutes. Allow to cool fully.

Sand the surface with 400-grit sandpaper to provide a key for the paint. Brush a coat of dark brown acrylic over the entire surface and allow to dry. Then brush gold or copper metallic acrylic over the top and immediately wipe back with a cloth, leaving metallic paint on the raised surfaces and dark paint in the recessed suture lines. This dry-brush technique creates the aged fossil look. Allow to dry, then apply one coat of clear acrylic varnish.
Add a small polymer clay crystal cluster in the centre of the spiral by pressing pointed teardrop shapes of translucent clay before curing.
Make the pendant in red oxide or deep brown clay and leave unpainted for a natural stone appearance.
Build an oversized version 60 mm across and mount it on a cord as a statement necklace centrepiece.

rows.problem: Spiral uncoils during handling before curing
rows.cause: Clay was too soft and the coils did not press firmly together
rows.fix: Press each coil firmly down against the previous one as you wind. Chill the clay briefly before curing if it is too soft to hold shape.

rows.problem: Suture lines are too faint after painting
rows.cause: Lines were not scored deeply enough before curing
rows.fix: Score to a minimum 1 mm depth. Deeper lines hold more dark paint and read more clearly after the gold wipe-back.

rows.problem: Spiral has a lumpy, uneven surface
rows.cause: The tapered rope was not smooth before coiling
rows.fix: Roll the rope slowly and evenly before coiling. A smooth rope produces a smooth coil face.
